patient_utterances: Hello?

[Clinician asked: "Hello? Hello? I can hear you, can you hear me? . Excellent, just before I go any further, can you confirm your name and your age, please?"] Hi. Can you hear me? Yeah, I can hear you now. Uh, yeah. My name is Jessica Smith, and I'm nineteen years old.

[Clinician asked: "OK. And how can I help you Mrs Smith?"] Um, yeah so uh, I mean this is, this is a bit embarrassing, but it's, it's  uh, hurting when I, , when I use the bathroom, when I pee. And um, it's been going on for a few days, so I'm a, I'm a little bit worried about it. Uh, not sure what to do.

[Clinician asked: "Right, well , I, thank you for coming in, so hopefully we can help with your symptoms. Um, so you mentioned it started a few days ago. Um, so, can you tell me a bit more about that, what kind of pain it is, that you're experiencing?"] Um, it's, it's not too bad, it's sort of like a stinging kind of pain. Um. It's not like unbearable, but uh, it's , it's definitely uh, it definitely bothers me when I pee. Um. And uh, yeah I also maybe sometimes get some like, pain kind of lower down in my tummy, when I, when I go to the bathroom. Uh, yeah.

[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>, OK. And, and, uh, the pain in your tummy, is it, can you describe the , the pain to me? Is it cramping, burning, sharp?"] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>, yeah, I don't know maybe uh, sort of, sort of cramping. I'm not really sure, but it's not like, it's not like cramping like when I get, when I have a period or something. It's like, I don't know, .
[Clinician asked: "Right, right, OK, OK. Um, fine. And, have you found yourself going to the toilet more often than normal?"] Um, not, I don't think so, I haven't noticed that.

[Clinician asked: "No, OK, fine. Have you noticed any, any funny colour to your urine? So, has there been, uh, , any blood in your urine? Have you noticed any other darkening of urine?"] Um, there was a little bit of blood earlier today, um, but besides that, not at all, no. Um. It does kind of, it, it kind of like, doesn't smell very, it smells a bit weird I guess, um, but, no, not really anything uh, anything drastic.

[Clinician asked: "Yes. <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> OK, fine, OK. Um, right, let me just , write a few notes down as well. Um. , and you mentioned a tummy pain, that also started a few days ago. Does the pain move anywhere else? Does it move towards the back, or towards your flank, the sides?"] No, not really, no.
[Clinician asked: "No, OK, fine. With those symptoms, have you noticed any other symptoms like nausea, vomiting?"] Uh, no, not , not at all.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Any fevers, or temperatures?"] think so.
[Clinician asked: ", sorry, was there any, any fevers or temperatures? Sorry, I didn't hear you."] No, no, no fever, no.
[Clinician asked: "No fever, OK, fine, sorry. Um, OK. Um, in terms of your bowel, are your bowels working OK?"] Um, uh, I think so, yeah, it seems <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>.
[Clinician asked: "So, uh, by that I mean, um, any, any diarrhoea, loose stool?"] Uh, no, not that I've, not that I've noticed at all.

[Clinician asked: "OK, all right. And um, Jessica, sometimes we have to ask very private sensitive questions, uh, just to , get a full history. Um, hope you don't mind me asking, but are you currently sexually active?"] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> Uh, yeah I am.
[Clinician asked: "OK, is that with a regular, partner?"] Ohh, yeah, so I, I've had a boyfriend for about a, a couple of months now, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "Fine, OK. Um, have you noticed any other discharge from, from down below?"] I haven't noticed anything.
[Clinician asked: "No, OK. When was your last um, uh, last STD screen? If, if ever?"] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> Don't think I've ever, I don't think I've ever had one.
[Clinician asked: "No, OK, fine. And, and um. Do you use any form of contraception, at all?"] Uh, yeah, we use condoms.
[Clinician asked: "You use condoms, OK. And when was your last, period?"] Uh, couple of weeks ago.
[Clinician asked: "Three weeks ago, OK. Is there any chance you could be pregnant perhaps?"] Say again.
[Clinician asked: "Is there <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> any chance you could be pregnant?"] Uh, I don't think so, I don't.
[Clinician asked: "No, no. , it's always worth asking uh, young females like yourself, um. Uh, um, so if that obviously you are worried about the pain."] Yeah.

[Clinician asked: "Um, not getting any better, uh, then I'd always , advise getting a pregnancy test, to rule out pregnancy. OK? Um, fine. So just moving on, moving on. Are you otherwise fit and well?"] Uh-huh. Um, yeah, I think I'm, I'm feeling OK, otherwise, yeah.

[Clinician asked: "And so by that I mean, do you have any other medical problems, medical <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>, medical history?"] Um, so, in the past I've had some uh, some IBS. Um. But, other than that I think no like uh, real serious medical problems that seem, yeah.

[Clinician asked: "OK. Do you take any regular medications?"] Uh, no, I did uh, I did uh, a little bit ago, or a couple of days ago, try some uh, over the counter bicarbonate, to try and help with the pain but it wasn't, it wasn't really helping.
[Clinician asked: "OK. So you've tried that and it didn't help? OK, all right. Um, do you have any allergies to any medications?"] Uh, yeah, I'm allergic to, Clindamycin.
[Clinician asked: "And what happens when you take Clindamycin?"] Um, I, I get some like a, a, a swelling, basically.

[Clinician asked: "Right then, OK. So it's quite a, seems like a quite severe allergy to it, is , is that right? Um, OK. Fine. In terms of your family history, is there anything I should be aware of, um, related to kidneys, bladder, bowels, guts?"] Yeah. Uh, no I don't think so, not that I, not anything I know about.

[Clinician asked: "OK, all right. And tell me a bit more about your home situation. Who's lives, who's at home with you?"] Um, so I live in a, live in a flat near my college, with a, a couple of like friends of mine. Uh, so I'm, I'm not like living at home or anything. Um.
[Clinician asked: "OK, and what <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>."] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> about sorry.
[Clinician asked: "OK, and are you a, are you working at the moment, or are you a student?"] I'm a student.
[Clinician asked: "OK, and what are you studying?"] Uh, I'm studying biology.
[Clinician asked: "And how's that going?"] Um, it's good yeah, it's really, it's really interesting uh, learning about, learning about how uh, how the world works, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> great. Do you have any aspirations for the future, in terms of careers?"] Uh, I don't know, maybe a doctor someday, you never know.

[Clinician asked: ", recommend it, we need doctors. Um, what about smoking and alcohol? Do you drink much in the way of, <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>, do you, do you smoke, or do you drink much in the way of alcohol?"] Uh, I don't, I don't smoke, uh, I do drink, like on the weekends, yeah.
[Clinician asked: ", so socially, OK. All right. Um, right, excellent. So, um, at this stage I , I would proceed to examination."] Yeah.

[Clinician asked: "But um, based on your story, um, just to summarise really. So, last three days, you've been having bit of burning pain on passing urine. A bit of, crampy tummy pain lower down. Um, , essentially but, but no other fevers or back pain, uh, if I, if I understand correctly."] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>

[Clinician asked: "I think probably do have what we call a simple UTI or urinary tract infection, bladder infection, um, which can be readily treated. So, I'll give you a course of antibiotics today."] Mm-hmm. OK.
[Clinician asked: "Something, called either Nitrofurantoin, or Trimethoprim. Uh, and it's normally a three day course. Uh, so, one tablet, twice a day, for three days."] OK.

[Clinician asked: "And , that should hopefully help your symptoms, but I also advise you to drink plenty of fluids. Let's try and flush that, you know, that infection out of your body, if you can."] OK.

[Clinician asked: "Um, you can try, cranberry juice, that can sometimes help with your symptoms. Um, and if you're still not better, by the end of this week, I want you to give me a call or come back and see me. And uh, we can have another look at your symptoms."] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> OK, so  three days of the medication, and then wait a little bit, if it still doesn't go away, call back, that OK?

[Clinician asked: "OK. Yeah, yeah absolutely. And things to look out for would be, you know, if you are having kind of, high temperatures, a back pain, um just , generally feeling more unwell. Uh, you know, I do want you give me a call back, 'cause then we do worry whether your infection may be spreading up towards yo"] OK. OK, is there, is there anything like, like, should I be worried about? Like, like, not having sex or anything within the next week, or anything like this?

[Clinician asked: "Yeah I mean if you can try and avoid it, I think that'd be sensible. Um, uh, <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> it's great that you're using barrier contraception. I would recommend you to continue using barrier contraception, but if you can avoid it while your symptoms clear up. That'd probably be the best for yo"] OK, OK. Uh, that, that makes sense. Ohh, OK, thank you so much.
[Clinician asked: "All right. Great. Thank you very much. Wish you all the best."] Yeah, thank you. Take care.
gold_note:
3/7 hx of dysuria and suprapubic pain. Nil frequency. Brief episode of haematuria, now resolved. Foul smelling.
Abdo pain - cramping, nil radiating 
Nil nausea/vomiting. Nil fever. Nil bowel sx
Currently sexually active - with regular partner for 2/12. No previous STD screen. Nil PV discharge. LMP 2/52 ago. 

PMH: IBS
DH: Nil regular 
Allergic to clindamycin 
FH: Nil regular 
SH: lives in a flat with friends, student in Biology, nil smoking, social EtOH at weekends

Imp: UTI/cystitis 

Plan:
1.	Nitrofurantoin abx for 3/7
2.	Push fluids
3.	Trial cranberry juice 
4.	Pregnancy test to exclude pregnancy 
5.	Review in 3d if no better, or earlier if worsening sx (fevers, back pain, vomiting, more unwell)

gold_sections.HIGHLIGHTS:
dysuria
suprapubic pain
haematuria
IBS
UTI/cystitis

naive.response_text:
Dear Jessica,

I understand that the pain you've been experiencing when urinating has been concerning and uncomfortable. Based on your symptoms—such as the stinging pain, lower abdominal cramps, and the presence of blood in your urine—it's likely a urinary tract infection (UTI), which is common and treatable. The antibiotics I've prescribed should help clear the infection, and I recommend drinking plenty of fluids to flush your system. Cranberry juice might also help alleviate your symptoms.

Regarding your question about sexual activity, it's best to avoid it until your symptoms improve to prevent further irritation or spreading the infection. Continue using condoms as you have been, but consider taking a break from sexual activity for now.

If your symptoms don't improve within a week or if you experience fever, back pain, or worsening symptoms, please don't hesitate to reach out or come back for a follow-up. You're taking the right steps, and with this treatment, you should feel better soon. Take care of yourself and let me know if you need anything else.

Warm regards,  
[Your Name]  
[Your Title]

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your concerns with me, Jessica. It’s completely understandable that you’re feeling worried and a bit embarrassed about these symptoms. Having pain when you pee and seeing blood can be unsettling, but please know that this is something we can work through together.

Based on what you’ve described—burning pain, some lower abdominal cramping, and a little blood in your urine—it sounds like you might have a urinary tract infection (UTI). UTIs are quite common, especially in young women, and are typically easily treatable with antibiotics. I’ll prescribe you a course of either Nitrofurantoin or Trimethoprim, which are standard treatments for this kind of infection. You’ll take one tablet twice a day for three days. 

To help flush the infection out, it’s important to drink plenty of fluids. You might also consider trying cranberry juice, which some people find helpful for UTIs. If your symptoms don’t improve by the end of this week, or if you experience any high fever, back pain, or feel more unwell, please give me a call or come back for a follow-up visit.

In the meantime, I recommend avoiding sexual activity until you feel better, just to give your body a chance to heal. It’s great that you’re using condoms, so please continue that practice. 

Before you leave, could you summarize what we’ve discussed today? That way, I can make sure everything is clear to you. We’ll plan to check in with you in about a week to see how you’re doing.

full.response_text:
I can hear how much this is worrying you — you mentioned, "I'm a little bit worried about it," and also said, "this is, this is a bit embarrassing." I understand how that might feel.

Painful urination, or pain when urinating, can be caused by a urinary tract infection (UTI). Given your symptoms like stinging pain, blood in urine, and lower abdominal pain, we should check for a UTI. The guidelines suggest that for men, treatment might involve antibiotics, but the exact approach can vary based on the specific situation.

To figure out what's going on, we'll need to do some tests. This might include a urinalysis and possibly a urine culture to check for infections. Understanding your symptoms fully will help us create a plan together.

Once we have the test results, we can recommend treatments to help relieve your pain. This might include medications or other therapies depending on what we find.
